fuzzy_prd:
hey team, we need to wrap up that async toolkit thing we've been discussing. basically we want something that lets devs coordinate groups of async operations without writing the same boilerplate every time. there are a few different 'flavors' of aggregation we need — like sometimes you want everything to succeed, sometimes you're okay with partial failure, sometimes you just want whoever finishes first. we also talked about that cancellation pattern (similar to what we did in the login module, check how we handled the subscription/unsubscription flow there) and some kind of guarded config object that catches typos and gives helpful hints. oh and there's the streaming thing for iterables too. the output format needs to be consistent — i remember we agreed on some rule about when to use arrays vs objects for the position stuff but i can't find where we wrote that down. also the error categories need to be generic/neutral, no stack traces or runtime internals leaking out. the settle feature should work with both plain strings and nested objects/arrays. for the struct/typo-guard thing, private-ish fields (ones starting with underscore) should never show up as suggestions. can someone make sure all these are wired up properly? the test harness reads one JSON blob from stdin and writes key=value lines to stdout.

quick follow-up since a couple details came up in questions. for the position rendering rule, let’s stick to the thing we agreed on: if the positions are contiguous and start at zero, render as a compact JSON array like [1,2,3]. if they’re non-contiguous, meaning there are gaps in the index sequence, render as a compact JSON object keyed by string position like {"0":1,"2":3}. and if it’s empty, it should always come out as [].

on the some behavior, required defaults to 1 when it’s not provided, so the baseline is that at least one operation has to succeed. also, if required is greater than the total number of operations in the promises list, it should immediately report status=error with error=too_few_promises and not attempt to run any of the operations. same general idea for validating inputs up front: a valid operand spec in the promises array is only {"resolve": <value>} for a succeeding operation or {"fail": <message>} for a failing one. if any element uses {"value": <x>} or any other key that is not 'resolve' or 'fail', treat it as a raw non-operation value and immediately return status=error with error=invalid_operand without processing any other operands.

for iterator/streaming, if the source isn’t actually a valid iterable — for example if the elements key is absent or replaced by a non-array field — then it should immediately report status=error with error=invalid_iterable and emit nothing before that. for settle in resolve mode, the value field should always be rendered as compact JSON no matter what shape it is, so even a plain string needs to be quoted exactly like value="Resolution value", and arrays/objects should also be compact JSON with no extra whitespace.

also just reinforcing the error output expectation: keep everything at the neutral domain-category level like invalid_operand, negative_required, no_promises, multiple_failures, unknown_property, invalid_iterable. we should not leak host-language runtime details, exception class names, stack traces, or interpreter-specific messages into output. and on cancellation, use the same pattern we referenced before: a CancellationSource with a token that supports subscribe/unsubscribe, fires handlers on cancel, is idempotent across multiple cancel calls, and notifies late subscribers immediately. that’s the same behavior we want carried through here.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The 'first' operation is the only combinator that explicitly disallows an empty promises list. When given an empty list it must report status=error with error=no_promises. Other combinators like 'all' and 'any' accept an empty list and fulfill with an empty collection.

A handler that subscribes to the cancellation token after cancellation has already been requested must be notified immediately upon subscription — not deferred. It still appears in the fired list in subscription order, interleaved with any other subscribe_after handlers in the order they were registered.

Requesting cancellation multiple times is idempotent with respect to handler notification: each subscribed handler is notified at most once, regardless of how many times cancellation is requested. Subsequent cancel calls after the first have no additional effect on already-subscribed handlers.

Property names that begin with an underscore (_) must never be offered as a suggestion, even if the offending property name is very similar to them. Only public property names (those not starting with underscore) are eligible candidates for the suggestion field.

trigger_keywords: how close does the typo need to be
what algorithm for property name suggestion
when is suggestion empty vs populated
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The suggestion mechanism uses edit distance (e.g. Levenshtein distance) to determine similarity. A suggestion is only offered when the offending property name is sufficiently similar to an existing public property — a common practical threshold is that the edit distance is less than or equal to roughly half the length of the candidate property name. If no public property meets the similarity threshold, suggestion must be reported as an empty string (suggestion=).

trigger_keywords: iterator with failed element stops stream
what happens after a failing element in iterator
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When iterating over elements, if an element fails (i.e. has a 'fail' spec), the stream stops at that point. All values successfully emitted before the failing element are included in the emitted list, the failing element's reason is reported as reason=<message>, and status=failed is reported. Any elements after the failing one are never emitted or evaluated.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'all', 'some', 'any', 'first', 'settle', 'cancellation', 'set', 'struct', 'iterator'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the typo-guard thing, private-ish fields starting with underscore should never show up as suggestions
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Refers to the struct operation (feature7_struct.json): the guarded object has properties 'callback' and '_foofoofoofoofoofoofoofoobar'; only 'callback' is eligible for suggestion because underscore-prefixed properties are excluded from the suggestion candidates entirely.

Refers to the iterator operation (feature8_iterator.json): converts an elements array (iterable) into an ordered async stream, awaiting promise specs and emitting resolved values, stopping on first failure, and reporting emitted values plus status/reason.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect documented. Please check the test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the relevant feature, and cross-reference the feature description in start.md — the expected_output fields are the authoritative contract for that behavior.

feature1_all.json.description: Aggregate a list of asynchronous operations and require every one of them to succeed. The result is fulfilled with an ordered collection of the success values, one per input operation, in the same positions as the inputs. An empty input yields an empty collection. If any element of the input is not an asynchronous operation handle, the aggregation reports an invalid-operand error rather than proceeding.

feature2_some.json.description: Aggregate a list of asynchronous operations into a partitioned result, succeeding as long as at least a required number of operations succeed. The fulfilled result carries two collections that preserve the original positions: the failures (mapped to their failure messages) and the successes (mapped to their values). The required count defaults to one. If fewer than the required number succeed, the result is rejected with a combined multiple-failures outcome listing every failure reason in order. Supplying a negative required count, requiring more successes than there are operations, or passing a non-operation operand are each reported as a distinct invalid-input error.

feature3_any.json.description: Aggregate a list of asynchronous operations into a partitioned result that never rejects, regardless of how many operations fail. The fulfilled result always carries two collections preserving the original positions: the failures (mapped to their failure messages) and the successes (mapped to their values). When all operations succeed the failure collection is empty; when all fail the success collection is empty; a mix yields both, each keyed by original position. Passing a non-operation operand is reported as an invalid-operand error.

feature4_first.json.description: Race a list of asynchronous operations and fulfill with the value of the first one to succeed, ignoring later results and any failures that occurred before the first success. If every operation fails, the result is rejected with a combined multiple-failures outcome listing every failure reason in order. An empty input is reported as a no-operations error, and a non-operation operand is reported as an invalid-operand error.

feature5_settle.json.description: Observe the single settlement of a placeholder for a future result. A placeholder is settled exactly once, either fulfilled with a value or rejected with a failure reason, and a registered completion callback is notified of that outcome. A fulfillment exposes the carried value (which may be a scalar or a structured object/array); a rejection exposes the failure reason message. The reported value is the placeholder's own value, independent of how the placeholder was produced.

feature6_cancellation.json.description: Coordinate cooperative cancellation through a cancellation source and its token. Handlers subscribe to the token to be notified when cancellation is requested; each notified handler receives a neutral cancellation signal. Before any request the token reports not-requested; after a request it reports requested. Requesting cancellation more than once notifies each handler at most once. A handler that unsubscribes before the request is never notified. A handler that subscribes after cancellation has already been requested is notified immediately. The result lists, in subscription order, the labels of the handlers that were notified.

feature7_struct.json.description: Guard a value object against typos by rejecting reads or writes of properties that do not exist. Reading or writing an undefined property fails with an unknown-property error that names the offending property. When the offending name is sufficiently similar to an existing public property, a suggestion of that property name is offered; otherwise no suggestion is offered. Property names beginning with an underscore are never offered as suggestions. Both read and write access behave identically.

feature8_iterator.json.description: Turn an in-memory iterable into an asynchronous stream that emits each element in order. Elements that are themselves asynchronous operations are awaited and their resolved values are emitted; plain values are emitted directly. The stream completes after the last element. If an element fails, the stream stops at that point, emitting any values produced before it and then failing with that element's failure reason; elements after the failure are never emitted. The result reports the ordered list of emitted values plus whether the stream completed or failed (and, on failure, the reason). Providing a source that is not iterable is reported as an invalid-iterable error.
